entry.title: Caution when getting other analyses in an LLVM pass

link.title: Caution when getting other analyses in an LLVM pass

entry.content:
Recently, I encountered a weird bug that when calling to getAnalysis<...> from an LLVM function pass, my program crashes!

Debugging a crash bug has never been considered easy in C++! Here is the problematic code:

bool MyFuncPass::runOnFunction(Function &F) {
  DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(F).getDomTree();
  ...
  return true;
}

It had baffled me for several weeks, since I did declare the usage of the DominatorTreeWrapperPass in the function getAnalysisUsage.

struct MyFuncPass : public FunctionPass {
  static char ID;
  MyFuncPass() : FunctionPass(ID) {}

  void getAnalysisUsage(AnalysisUsage &AU) const override {
    AU.addRequired<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>();
  }

  virtual bool runOnFunction(Function &F) override;
};

Moreover, when turning this function pass into a module pass, everything is fine!

struct MyModuPass : public ModulePass {
  static char ID;
  MyModuPass() : ModulePass(ID) {}

  void getAnalysisUsage(AnalysisUsage &AU) const override {
    AU.addRequired<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>();
  }

  virtual bool runOnModule(Function &F) override;
};

bool MyModuPass::runOnModule(Module &M) {
  for (Function &F: M) {
    // using the same call as in MyFuncPass::runOnFunction
    // but here, it works, suprisingly!
    DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(F).getDomTree();
  }

  return true;
}

After spending quite a lot of effort on googling, StackOverflow, reading LLVM mailing list and source code, finally, I realized the problem is that I did not call getAnalysis correctly in the function pass.

In particular:

  • For a module pass, to obtain the dominator tree of a function F, you can call:

    // call inside a module pass
DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(F).getDomTree();

  • But, for a function pass, you should not pass the function F as an argument for getAnalysis (if you do, your program will crash!). So, the following code is enough:

    // call inside a function pass
DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>().getDomTree();

So, the final code for a function pass that works for me is something like below:

struct MyFuncPass : public FunctionPass {
  static char ID;
  MyFuncPass() : FunctionPass(ID) {}

  void getAnalysisUsage(AnalysisUsage &AU) const override {
    AU.addRequired<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>();
  }

  virtual bool runOnFunction(Function &F) override;
};

bool MyFuncPass::runOnFunction(Function &F) {
  // Remember to NOT pass F to getAnalysis. No crash with this code!
  DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>().getDomTree();
  ...
  return true;
}

Magical!!! Right?

It took me a few weeks to finally realize it!
entry.summary: Recently, I encountered a weird bug that when calling to getAnalysis<...> from an LLVM function pass, my program crashes! Debugging a crash bug has never been considered easy in C++! Here is the problematic code: bool MyFuncPass::runOnFunction(Function &F) { DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(F).getDomTree(); ... return true; } It had baffled me for several weeks, since I did declare the usage of the DominatorTreeWrapperPass in the function getAnalysisUsage. struct MyFuncPass : public FunctionPass { static char ID; MyFuncPass() : FunctionPass(ID) {} void getAnalysisUsage(AnalysisUsage &AU) const override { AU.addRequired<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(); } virtual bool runOnFunction(Function &F) override; }; Moreover, when turning this function pass into a module pass, everything is fine! struct MyModuPass : public ModulePass { static char ID; MyModuPass() : ModulePass(ID) {} void getAnalysisUsage(AnalysisUsage &AU) const override { AU.addRequired<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(); } virtual bool runOnModule(Function &F) override; }; bool MyModuPass::runOnModule(Module &M) { for (Function &F: M) { // using the same call as in MyFuncPass::runOnFunction // but here, it works, suprisingly! DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(F).getDomTree(); } return true; } After spending quite a lot of effort on googling, StackOverflow, reading LLVM mailing list and source code, finally, I realized the problem is that I did not call getAnalysis correctly in the function pass. In particular: For a module pass, to obtain the dominator tree of a function F, you can call: // call inside a module pass DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(F).getDomTree(); But, for a function pass, you should not pass the function F as an argument for getAnalysis (if you do, your program will crash!). So, the following code is enough: // call inside a function pass DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>().getDomTree(); So, the final code for a function pass that works for me is something like below: struct MyFuncPass : public FunctionPass { static char ID; MyFuncPass() : FunctionPass(ID) {} void getAnalysisUsage(AnalysisUsage &AU) const override { AU.addRequired<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>(); } virtual bool runOnFunction(Function &F) override; }; bool MyFuncPass::runOnFunction(Function &F) { // Remember to NOT pass F to getAnalysis. No crash with this code! DominatorTree &DT = getAnalysis<DominatorTreeWrapperPass>().getDomTree(); ... return true; } Magical!!! Right? It took me a few weeks to finally realize it!

entry.title: Writing safely extensible OCaml code
entry.content:
This code is not safely extensible

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | _ -> false

This is because when extending expr with a new construct String of string, it is easily to forget to extend the equal_expr function due to no warning from the compiler.

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float
  | String of string

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | _ -> false          (* no warning here since _ matches all other cases_*)

Better practice, explicitly enumerate all pattern matching cases:

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float

let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Integer _, _ -> false
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | Float _, _ -> false

When extending expr with a new construct, the compiler will report warning if the equal_expr is not extended.

type expr =
  | Integer of int64
  | Float of float
  | String of string

(* There will be a warning report at this function! *)
let equal_expr e1, e2 =
  match e1, e2 with
  | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2
  | Integer _, _ -> false
  | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2
  | Float _, _ -> false
entry.summary: This code is not safely extensible type expr = | Integer of int64 | Float of float let equal_expr e1, e2 = match e1, e2 with | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2 | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2 | _ -> false This is because when extending expr with a new construct String of string, it is easily to forget to extend the equal_expr function due to no warning from the compiler. type expr = | Integer of int64 | Float of float | String of string let equal_expr e1, e2 = match e1, e2 with | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2 | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2 | _ -> false (* no warning here since _ matches all other cases_*) Better practice, explicitly enumerate all pattern matching cases: type expr = | Integer of int64 | Float of float let equal_expr e1, e2 = match e1, e2 with | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2 | Integer _, _ -> false | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2 | Float _, _ -> false When extending expr with a new construct, the compiler will report warning if the equal_expr is not extended. type expr = | Integer of int64 | Float of float | String of string (* There will be a warning report at this function! *) let equal_expr e1, e2 = match e1, e2 with | Integer i1, Integer i2 -> i1 = i2 | Integer _, _ -> false | Float f1, Float f2 -> Float.equal f1 f2 | Float _, _ -> false

entry.title: Auto close distracting apps (i.e., Chrome, Firefox)

link.title: Auto close distracting apps (i.e., Chrome, Firefox)

entry.content:
Have you ever felt like want to surf the web for 5 mins, and end up spending hours on your web browsers?

If yes, then these small Shell scripts may be a bit of help. They check every 30s if Google Chrome, Firefox, or Safari is running to kill them all immediately :-)

  • For macOS: auto close distracting apps in macOS

  • For Linux: auto close distracting apps in Linux
entry.summary: Have you ever felt like want to surf the web for 5 mins, and end up spending hours on your web browsers? If yes, then these small Shell scripts may be a bit of help. They check every 30s if Google Chrome, Firefox, or Safari is running to kill them all immediately :-) For macOS: auto close distracting apps in macOS For Linux: auto close distracting apps in Linux

entry.title: Emacs out of the box
entry.content:
Some Emacs applications that make you smile (or smile out loud 😉).

  • Playing Spotify on Emacs: Spotify client, Helm Spotify, Spotify Spacemacs layer.
entry.summary: Some Emacs applications that make you smile (or smile out loud 😉). Playing Spotify on Emacs: Spotify client, Helm Spotify, Spotify Spacemacs layer.

entry.title: OCaml programs might crash in the bytecode mode

link.title: OCaml programs might crash in the bytecode mode

entry.content:
Recently, I encountered a weird error on OCaml that my program could crash due to a mysterious error Fatal error: executable program file not found. I couldn’t debug or trace this issue.

./discover test.c

Compiling file: test.c

Start to analyze...

Fatal error: executable program file not found
Aborted (core dumped)

It turned down that it is related to the module ocaml/runtime/backtrace_byt.c, as described here.

Still, I have no idea why OCaml didn’t throw an exception, but killed my program instead. I further discovered that this crash happens only in the bytecode compilation mode. In the native mode, proper exceptions will be thrown like below:

./discover test.c

Compiling file: test.c

Start to analyze...

Exception: (Failure int_of_string)
Raised by primitive operation at file "src/llvm/llir.ml", line 498, characters 8-55
Called from file "src/llvm/llir.ml", line 501, characters 23-67
...
Called from file "src/frontend/discover.ml", line 74, characters 4-48
entry.summary: Recently, I encountered a weird error on OCaml that my program could crash due to a mysterious error Fatal error: executable program file not found. I couldn’t debug or trace this issue. ./discover test.c Compiling file: test.c Start to analyze... Fatal error: executable program file not found Aborted (core dumped) It turned down that it is related to the module ocaml/runtime/backtrace_byt.c, as described here. Still, I have no idea why OCaml didn’t throw an exception, but killed my program instead. I further discovered that this crash happens only in the bytecode compilation mode. In the native mode, proper exceptions will be thrown like below: ./discover test.c Compiling file: test.c Start to analyze... Exception: (Failure int_of_string) Raised by primitive operation at file "src/llvm/llir.ml", line 498, characters 8-55 Called from file "src/llvm/llir.ml", line 501, characters 23-67 ... Called from file "src/frontend/discover.ml", line 74, characters 4-48

entry.title: Why does LLVM handle dynamic and static struct similarly?

link.title: Why does LLVM handle dynamic and static struct similarly?

entry.content:
I have no idea, but I just discovered that when compiling by Clang to LLVM IR, the generated code of statically and dynamically allocated data structures are quite similar.

Here is the sample C code:

#include<stdio.h>
#include<stdlib.h>

struct foo {
  int x;
  int y;
};

int main(int argc, char **argv) {
  struct foo a;
  a.x = 1;

  struct foo* b = malloc(sizeof(struct foo));
  b->x = 2;

  return 3;
}

Here is output LLVM IR, compiled by Clang:

%v3 = alloca %struct.foo, align 4
%v5 = getelementptr inbounds %struct.foo, %struct.foo* %v3, i32 0, i32 0
store i32 1, i32* %v5, align 4, !tbaa !2
%v6 = call noalias i8* @malloc(i64 8) #3
%v7 = bitcast i8* %v6 to %struct.foo*
%v8 = getelementptr inbounds %struct.foo, %struct.foo* %v7, i32 0, i32 0
store i32 2, i32* %v8, align 4, !tbaa !2
ret i32 3

In the result, the two statements a.x = 1; are b->x = 2; are compiled into very similar output statements: getelementptr for %v5 and %v8.
entry.summary: I have no idea, but I just discovered that when compiling by Clang to LLVM IR, the generated code of statically and dynamically allocated data structures are quite similar. Here is the sample C code: #include<stdio.h> #include<stdlib.h> struct foo { int x; int y; }; int main(int argc, char **argv) { struct foo a; a.x = 1; struct foo* b = malloc(sizeof(struct foo)); b->x = 2; return 3; } Here is output LLVM IR, compiled by Clang: %v3 = alloca %struct.foo, align 4 %v5 = getelementptr inbounds %struct.foo, %struct.foo* %v3, i32 0, i32 0 store i32 1, i32* %v5, align 4, !tbaa !2 %v6 = call noalias i8* @malloc(i64 8) #3 %v7 = bitcast i8* %v6 to %struct.foo* %v8 = getelementptr inbounds %struct.foo, %struct.foo* %v7, i32 0, i32 0 store i32 2, i32* %v8, align 4, !tbaa !2 ret i32 3 In the result, the two statements a.x = 1; are b->x = 2; are compiled into very similar output statements: getelementptr for %v5 and %v8.

entry.title: Who can beat a Linux with an Xfce desktop environment?

link.title: Who can beat a Linux with an Xfce desktop environment?

entry.content:
Have been using many versions of all of three major operating systems Windows, Linux, Mac OS, my conclusion is that a Linux with an Xfce desktop environment is the best in many aspects: efficiency, elegance, user friendly, customizability, etc.

Seriously, as of 2019, which operating systems can consume around 1GB of RAM after rebooting like my Linux Mint Xfce 19.2?
entry.summary: Have been using many versions of all of three major operating systems Windows, Linux, Mac OS, my conclusion is that a Linux with an Xfce desktop environment is the best in many aspects: efficiency, elegance, user friendly, customizability, etc. Seriously, as of 2019, which operating systems can consume around 1GB of RAM after rebooting like my Linux Mint Xfce 19.2?

entry.title: Printing PDF files with preview in Linux by using Google Chrome

link.title: Printing PDF files with preview in Linux by using Google Chrome

entry.content:
Have been using Linux for more than a decade, I also have been struggling all this time to find a good PDF reader which can support printing preview and multiple-page printing mode.

It’s quite disappointing that native Linux applications like Evince or Okular do not support printing preview, which is certainly important to check if documents are readable in the multiple-page printing mode.

I sometimes use Windows-based PDF readers like Foxit or PDF-XChange through Wine emulator. However, such tools are so unstable in the Wine environment, and they crash so often. Besides, PDF files must be opened directly from these applications; they cannot be opened through command-line, or right-mouse-click dialog.

Such problems are gone after I discover that Google Chrome can help to print PDF files in multiple-page mode and with printing preview. Moreover, I can just easily open PDF file from the right-mouse-click dialog, or even from command line. Yay!

Long live Google Chrome.
entry.summary: Have been using Linux for more than a decade, I also have been struggling all this time to find a good PDF reader which can support printing preview and multiple-page printing mode. It’s quite disappointing that native Linux applications like Evince or Okular do not support printing preview, which is certainly important to check if documents are readable in the multiple-page printing mode. I sometimes use Windows-based PDF readers like Foxit or PDF-XChange through Wine emulator. However, such tools are so unstable in the Wine environment, and they crash so often. Besides, PDF files must be opened directly from these applications; they cannot be opened through command-line, or right-mouse-click dialog. Such problems are gone after I discover that Google Chrome can help to print PDF files in multiple-page mode and with printing preview. Moreover, I can just easily open PDF file from the right-mouse-click dialog, or even from command line. Yay! Long live Google Chrome.

entry.title: Beautiful backtrace for C++
entry.content:
Debugging crashed or segmentation fault C++ program will never be more painful with the help of this awesome project backward-cpp.

The installation and usage of backward-cpp is very easy and straight forward. For my project, I installed it as a git-submodule:

git submodule add https://github.com/bombela/backward-cpp

and modified the CMakeLists.txt to add the following lines:

add_subdirectory(/path/to/backward-cpp)

# This will add backward.cpp to your target
add_executable(mytarget mysource.cpp ${BACKWARD_ENABLE})

# This will add libraries, definitions and include directories needed by backward
# by setting each property on the target.
add_backward(mytarget)

I also needed to install extra libraries (in Linux) to display the beautified backtrace:

sudo apt-get install libdw-dev libelf-dev libdwarf-dev

and finally, configure CMake to generate debugging information:

set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE Debug)
set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE RelWithDebInfo)
set(CMAKE_SHARED_LIBRARY_LINK_C_FLAGS "-rdynamic")
entry.summary: Debugging crashed or segmentation fault C++ program will never be more painful with the help of this awesome project backward-cpp. The installation and usage of backward-cpp is very easy and straight forward. For my project, I installed it as a git-submodule: git submodule add https://github.com/bombela/backward-cpp and modified the CMakeLists.txt to add the following lines: add_subdirectory(/path/to/backward-cpp) # This will add backward.cpp to your target add_executable(mytarget mysource.cpp ${BACKWARD_ENABLE}) # This will add libraries, definitions and include directories needed by backward # by setting each property on the target. add_backward(mytarget) I also needed to install extra libraries (in Linux) to display the beautified backtrace: sudo apt-get install libdw-dev libelf-dev libdwarf-dev and finally, configure CMake to generate debugging information: set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE Debug) set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE RelWithDebInfo) set(CMAKE_SHARED_LIBRARY_LINK_C_FLAGS "-rdynamic")

entry.title: Generate CMT and CMTI files for LLVM in Opam.

link.title: Generate CMT and CMTI files for LLVM in Opam.

entry.content:
The current implementation of OCaml binding for LLVM does not generate CMT and CMTI files. Unfortunately, these files are used by Merlin to get the type information of expressions, or to locate these expressions in the Ocaml-binding LLVM source code files

Here is my quick and temporary fix to generate these CMT and CMTI files.

cd ~/.opam/4.08.1/lib/llvm

for file in *.mli; do
  ocamlc $file -bin-annot;
done
entry.summary: The current implementation of OCaml binding for LLVM does not generate CMT and CMTI files. Unfortunately, these files are used by Merlin to get the type information of expressions, or to locate these expressions in the Ocaml-binding LLVM source code files Here is my quick and temporary fix to generate these CMT and CMTI files. cd ~/.opam/4.08.1/lib/llvm for file in *.mli; do ocamlc $file -bin-annot; done
